---
title: "Final Project (Non-Technical Report)"
author: "Acree Tejada and Anwar Gafar"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

**Please direct this report towards (hypothetical) stakeholders/investors such as people working in the hospitality management industry or hotel owners/managers. This report should be written in a way that is accessible to non-technical readers, so it should not include any code/technical jargon. Summarize the most important findings of your analysis and suggestions for predicting booking cancellations better. The goal of this report is to communicate the value of your analysis and insights to stakeholders who may not have a technical background, so focus on the practical implications of your findings and how they can be used to improve business outcomes. Write your response as plain text below.**

Introduction:

A business succeeds on its ability to understand what affects its demand and sources of revenue. In the hospitality industry, the overall hope is to house as many people as possible and fill as many rooms within the establishment.As technology has grown, the overwhelming method of choice to reserve these hotel rooms became online booking/reservations. The convenience of the method and its ability to display available rooms to potential customers was second to none.Where hotels run into issues however, comes in the uncertainty that exists if a customer decides to cancel a booked room in their establishment as that previously confirmed booking reduced the number of available rooms for other customers and involved planning on the hotels' staff regarding work hours and general operations.

Methodology:

The easiest way to find a solution came in the most accessible form, data about hotels. Analyzing the available data of around 3500 total observations, to find a reasonable way to predict these potential cancellations became the main goal. Any team of analysts understands the value that being able to predict future outcomes and results can provide to a sector like the hospitality industry. So we built a predictive model to show us two things, "The likelihood/probability of a hotel booking getting canceled" and secondly identifying what considerations the management and owners of hotels can do to get an advantage or tackle current concerns to their revenue.

We went through a set of models to obtain what we classify as the optimal model, the model best at predicting the likelihood of cancellations and were able to perform at a 0.8786 out of 1.000 level, meaning that our trained model would be able to understand external data or data relevant to specific hotels and use that to predict the probability of booking cancellations. In making this model, we combined a multitude of the variables in a standard hotel data set, for example, number of people staying(adults, children and babies), the duration of the stay(in terms of nights and days) and more interesting factors like deposit type and whether a certain person had previously cancelled their bookings. Having these sets of relevant factors allow us to capture a huge portion of information that is meant to help you plan around reducing the uncertainty that comes with booking cancellations.

Our Findings:

While we cannot provide direct advice about what your business should do, as that will be dependent on your resources and manpower. The important considerations that our model is able to give to you would be to focus on confirming more firmly the number of people that will be in a booked room, as larger families may tend to cancel more. An additional interest can be placed on your available system to take note of the room types you have available in certain periods of time as the more comfortable and luxurious room types available may be the reason why people choose to cancel their booking later on. The variable regarding previous cancellations by people is important to keep track of, as having more expectations regarding the payment of direct deposits works hand-in-hand with ensuring people honor their reservations. Finally, there was a specific impact of the duration of the persons stay in determining how likely they would be to cancel, the length of stay is likely to show evidence that shorter stays may push the needle more on whether people are more likely or less likely to cancel their hotel booking. The hope with our review of your hotel data is to provide a basis from which you can make decisions at present and for the future, with options to monetize gaps in the system still available.
